title: Free vs Paid AI Subtitle Tools
deck: Free AI subtitle tools look generous on the pricing page — then you hit the export wall, the watermark, or the queue. Here’s what free plans really include, when paid is worth it, and which tools respect a creator budget.
excerpt: Honest 2026 comparison of free vs paid AI subtitle tools: SRT export limits, watermarks, quotas, mobile pain, and when upgrading actually saves time and money.
author: Cutup Editorial

metaTitle: Free vs Paid AI Subtitle Tools (2026 Honest Comparison) — Cutup
metaDescription: What free AI subtitle tools actually allow in 2026 — SRT export, watermarks, quotas, mobile limits — and when paid subtitle software is worth the money.
ogTitle: Free vs Paid AI Subtitle Tools: What Creators Actually Get in 2026
ogDescription: Not marketing tiers — real limits on free subtitle generators, hidden costs, and the best value for creators.

faqSchema.question: Are free AI subtitle generators good enough?
faqSchema.answer: For occasional uploads and simple talking-head videos, yes — as a draft. For daily publishing, brand channels, or clean SRT export, free tiers usually hit walls quickly through quotas, watermarks, or paywalled exports.

faqSchema.question: Which subtitle tools allow free SRT export?
faqSchema.answer: Cutup includes SRT export on the free tier. Descript offers limited free transcription with export options. VEED and Kapwing typically gate clean SRT downloads behind paid plans. YouTube Studio auto-captions are free but inconsistent for file export.

faqSchema.question: Why do subtitle tools charge so much?
faqSchema.answer: Speech-to-text, GPU processing, storage, and browser-based editing are expensive at scale. Pricing reflects transcription minutes, export features, collaboration seats, and styled caption libraries — not just a text file.

faqSchema.question: Can I create subtitles without watermarks?
faqSchema.answer: Some free plans add watermarks on exports or burned-in captions. Paid tiers usually remove them. Always check export settings on the free plan before batching content.

faqSchema.question: Which subtitle tools work best on mobile?
faqSchema.answer: CapCut is strongest for mobile burn-in. Lightweight SRT tools like Cutup work well on mobile browsers for link-to-file workflows. Heavy browser editors (VEED, Kapwing) are often unreliable on phones.

faqSchema.question: Is it worth paying for AI subtitle software?
faqSchema.answer: Worth it when subtitle work is a weekly bottleneck — daily Shorts, client work, or multilingual tracks. Not worth it if you publish rarely and can tolerate manual cleanup on a free draft.

faqSchema.question: What is the hidden cost of free subtitle tools?
faqSchema.answer: Time lost to queues, failed exports, tool-switching, re-uploading after refresh, and fixing bad auto-captions manually — plus quota anxiety that slows your publishing rhythm.

faqSchema.question: How do I choose between free subtitle generators?
faqSchema.answer: Match the tool to your output: SRT file for an NLE, burned-in captions for Shorts, or transcript editing for podcasts. Test free export on a real video before committing your workflow.
